description: Type systems, type safety, and type inference in programming languages.

facts.context: A type system is sound when:
facts.truth: well-typed programs don't get stuck (no runtime type errors)
distractors: all programs type-check successfully without any errors reported
the compiler runs faster than interpretation would take
types are optional annotations that help with documentation only

facts.context: Hindley-Milner type inference (used in ML, Haskell) provides:
facts.truth: principal types without annotations via Algorithm W
distractors: dependent types with proof obligations that must be discharged
gradual typing with runtime checks inserted automatically
only monomorphic types requiring explicit annotations everywhere

facts.context: Covariance of generic types (List<Dog> assignable to List<Animal>) is:
facts.truth: safe for read-only but breaks for writes
distractors: always safe in all contexts regardless of read or write operations
never allowed in any statically typed programming language
only relevant for primitive numeric types and not objects

facts.context: The 'billion dollar mistake' (null references) is addressed by:
facts.truth: option types making nullability explicit in the type
distractors: garbage collection which automatically handles null pointers
exceptions that are thrown whenever null is accessed
runtime null checks that the compiler inserts automatically

facts.context: Structural typing (TypeScript, Go interfaces) determines compatibility by:
distractors: explicit implements/extends declarations required in source code
runtime reflection checking class hierarchies dynamically
name equality requiring identical type names across modules

facts.context: Dependent types (Idris, Agda) allow types to depend on:
facts.truth: values, enabling proofs in types (e.g., Vec n)
distractors: only other types but never on runtime values
nothing beyond what ML-style polymorphism provides
external configuration files loaded at compile time

facts.context: Gradual typing (TypeScript's any, Python's typing) provides:
facts.truth: optional static types with dynamic fallback
distractors: full static guarantees identical to languages like Haskell or Rust
no benefits over purely dynamic typing whatsoever
automatic type inference making annotations completely unnecessary

facts.context: Refinement types (Liquid Haskell) extend base types with:
distractors: runtime assertions that throw exceptions on violation
documentation comments with no semantic meaning
inheritance hierarchies for subtyping relationships

facts.context: Type erasure (Java generics, Haskell) means at runtime:
facts.truth: generic type parameters are not available
distractors: all type information is preserved for reflection
types are stored alongside values in memory
runtime type checks are automatically generated

facts.context: Liskov Substitution Principle for subtypes requires:
distractors: supertypes must implement all subtype methods as well
only single inheritance with no interfaces allowed
runtime type checks before every method call

facts.context: Row polymorphism (OCaml objects, PureScript records) enables:
facts.truth: extensible records with type-safe field access
distractors: only fixed-size tuples with numeric indexing
dynamic field addition without any compile-time checks
inheritance-based polymorphism exclusively

facts.context: Linear types (Rust ownership) enforce that values are:
facts.truth: used exactly once, preventing aliasing bugs
distractors: immutable and never modified after creation
garbage collected with reference counting
copied on every assignment automatically
